message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "Competitive landscape and market analysis for AI-powered recruitment platforms targeting solo/independent recruiters and small recruiting agencies (teams of 5-10), with a freemium model (free individual tier + paid team tier).

Research these dimensions specifically:
1. Existing recruitment platforms (ATS, recruiting CRM, applicant tracking) that target solo recruiters and small agencies — e.g. Recruit CRM, Loxo, Crelate, Manatal, JobAdder, Zoho Recruit, Bullhorn, RecruiterFlow, Vincere, iSmartRecruit, Ceipal, Tracker, Hireflow, hiring tools like Workable, Ashby, Greenhouse (note which are NOT for small/solo). For each relevant one: target customer, pricing/tiers, free tier availability, AI features, key strengths/weaknesses.
2. Which of these use a freemium or free-tier model (Zoho Recruit free plan, etc.) and how they monetize. How "easy to start" / self-serve onboarding is positioned.
3. Where AI is being applied in recruiting today (resume parsing, candidate matching/ranking, sourcing, outreach email generation, interview scheduling, candidate Q&A, job description writing, chrome extensions for sourcing from LinkedIn). Which features are table-stakes vs differentiators in 2025-2026.
4. The pain points of small/independent recruiters: data scattered across WhatsApp/Excel/Google Drive, time spent on admin, lack of context on past placements, no revenue visibility. What tools claim to solve these and gaps that remain.
5. Pricing benchmarks: typical per-seat/month pricing for recruiting CRM/ATS at the small-agency level, and what free tiers include.
6. Market size and trends for recruitment software / recruiting CRM, especially the SMB / independent recruiter segment."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://prepzo.ai/blog/recruiterflow-pricing
**Title:** Recruiterflow Pricing 2026: Real Cost Per Seat & Alternatives - Prepzo
**Found via:** practitioner pain points / gaps search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll fetch the page content first.

input.prompt: Extract all concrete, checkable claims about: RecruiterFlow pricing (per-seat/month, tiers, minimum seats, annual vs monthly), free tier availability, AI features, target customer (solo recruiters/small agencies), alternatives mentioned and their pricing, market trends. Include exact pricing numbers and direct quotes. Also note the publish date if available.

content.content:
# Recruiterflow Pricing Claims Extraction

**Publication Date:** May 7, 2026

## Recruiterflow Pricing Structure

**Three Tiers (Annual Billing):**
- Recruit: $99/user/month
- Recruit Pro: $129/user/month
- Recruit Enterprise: $159/user/month

**Minimum Commitment:** Three-seat minimum across all plans ($3,564/year minimum on Recruit tier)

**Key Quote:** "No solo plan. The smallest contract is three seats, even if only one recruiter logs in."

## Feature Gating

Email/SMS sequences and recruiting automation require Pro tier. AI sourcing ("Recruiterflow Sourcing") and SSO/SAML require Enterprise tier only.

**Key Quote:** "AI sourcing is Enterprise-only, which is a $60 per seat per month upgrade"

## Free Tier Availability

No free forever plan offered. The article states: "There is no solo plan and no free tier."

14-day free trial available with full feature access.

## Target Customers

Purpose-built for staffing agencies and recruiting firms. Explicitly not recommended for corporate in-house hiring teams. The article notes it "punishes solo recruiters and freelance desks" due to three-seat minimum.

## Alternatives Mentioned with Pricing

- **Bullhorn:** ~$150/seat (staffing accounts, climbs higher)
- **Loxo Basic:** $169/user/month (includes free tier option)
- **Ashby, Greenhouse, Lever:** Corporate-focused alternatives
- **JobAdder, Crelate, Manatal:** Agency alternatives referenced

## Real Cost Analysis

10-recruiter desk on Pro tier estimated at ~$40,000/year all-in when including third-party tools (Aircall: $40-70/user/month; LinkedIn Recruiter Lite: ~$170/user/month).

claims.claim: RecruiterFlow has no free tier and no solo plan; its smallest contract requires a three-seat minimum (~$3,564/year on the entry Recruit tier), making it unsuitable for solo/independent recruiters.
claims.quote: No solo plan. The smallest contract is three seats, even if only one recruiter logs in.

claims.claim: RecruiterFlow's three pricing tiers (annual billing) are Recruit at $99/user/month, Recruit Pro at $129/user/month, and Recruit Enterprise at $159/user/month.
RecruiterFlow gates AI sourcing behind its Enterprise tier only, representing roughly a $60/seat/month upgrade over the base plan.
claims.quote: AI sourcing is Enterprise-only, which is a $60 per seat per month upgrade

claims.claim: Among competing agency/recruiting CRM tools, Bullhorn runs around $150/seat and Loxo Basic is $169/user/month (with a free tier option), establishing small-agency per-seat pricing benchmarks.
claims.quote: Bullhorn: ~$150/seat ... Loxo Basic: $169/user/month (includes free tier option)

claims.claim: A 10-recruiter desk on RecruiterFlow's Pro tier costs roughly $40,000/year all-in once third-party tools (Aircall, LinkedIn Recruiter Lite) are included.
claims.quote: 10-recruiter desk on Pro tier estimated at ~$40,000/year all-in when including third-party tools

content.text: Extracted claims from the Prepzo blog post on RecruiterFlow pricing. The source is a vendor/competitor blog (Prepzo, an AI recruiting tool) with an inherent positioning bias against RecruiterFlow, so I rated it "blog" quality. Key findings centered on RecruiterFlow's lack of a free/solo tier and three-seat minimum, its tier pricing ($99/$129/$159 per user/month), Enterprise-gated AI sourcing, and competitor pricing benchmarks (Bullhorn ~$150/seat, Loxo Basic $169/user/month with free tier). Publish date: May 7, 2026.
